ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Chinese rojak. Mapped to 'salad food product' (FOODON_03311737) as the closest structural match for a mixed tossed salad-style dish. You tiao-specific term absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed dishes — salads and mixed vegetable dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (mixed salad dishes) used as closest defensible match. No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for Chinese rojak or you tiao-based mixed dishes as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chinese rojak or you tiao mixed dish. SNOMED CT does not cover this specific regional preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of Chinese rojak with you tiao is approximately 250 g, comprising a mixed plate of fried dough fritters, bean sprouts, cucumber, and pineapple tossed in prawn paste dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Rojak (You Tiao) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chinese Rojak (You Tiao), food code F08010019. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; value estimated from deep-fried dough composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried cruller/you tiao (NDB 18249) as a secondary reference for fat fraction breakdown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese rojak with you tiao in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 76 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate components: you tiao (deep-fried refined wheat dough, analogous to fried cruller/doughnut, GI ~76 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried dough products) and the high free-sugar content from prawn paste dressing (sugar 19.6g/100g). The pineapple component (GI ~59) and bean sprouts (negligible GI contribution) partially moderate the overall estimate, but the dominant refined-dough and free-sugar load supports a high GI classification. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (76 × 45.39 × 2.5) / 100 = 86.0g GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 792 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; a 250g serving delivers approximately 1,980 mg sodium, representing 99% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Chinese rojak with you tiao due to its very high sodium content from prawn paste (hae ko); if consumed, request minimal dressing and limit portion to no more than 50–80g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load driven by refined you tiao dough (carbohydrate 45.4g/100g), free sugar 19.6g/100g in prawn paste dressing, and low dietary fibre (2.15g/100g), resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this dish or limit to a very small tasting portion (under 80g), request significantly reduced sugar in the dressing, and pair with a low-GI food to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined cardiovascular risk from high sodium (792 mg/100g promoting hypertension), estimated trans fat (~0.5g/100g from deep-frying you tiao), saturated fat (~2.8g/100g), and high free sugar (19.6g/100g promoting dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Chinese rojak with you tiao due to its high sodium, trans fat from deep-fried you tiao, and high free-sugar content, all of which independently and synergistically elevate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; estimated trans fat ~0.5g/100g is pro-inflammatory; sodium 792mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to deep-fried AGEs, trans fat, and very high sodium, all of which promote systemic inflammation and joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Rojak with You Tiao is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker salad dish consisting of you tiao (deep-fried wheat dough fritters), fresh bean sprouts, sliced cucumber, and pineapple chunks, all tossed together in a thick, pungent prawn paste sauce (hae ko) sweetened with sugar and sharpened with chilli. The dish is served at room temperature and is characterised by a bold contrast of textures — crispy-chewy you tiao against crunchy raw vegetables and juicy pineapple — and a complex flavour profile combining savoury-fermented, sweet, and spicy notes. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 322 kcal/100g, with a high carbohydrate load (45.4g/100g) dominated by refined wheat starch from you tiao and free sugars (19.6g/100g) from the dressing. Dietary fibre is low at 2.15g/100g, and sodium is very high at 792mg/100g, primarily from fermented prawn paste. The deep-frying process introduces estimated trans fat (~0.5g/100g) and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). This dish is clinically significant for individuals managing hypertension (very high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high glycaemic load, high free sugar, low fibre), cardiovascular disease (sodium, trans fat, free sugar), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs, trans fat, sodium). It is not halal due to prawn paste and is not suitable for gluten-free diets. Chinese rojak is a distinctly Singaporean dish with Hokkien-Teochew culinary roots, commonly found at hawker centres and food courts across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in Chinese rojak?
Is Chinese rojak high in sugar?
Is Chinese rojak suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F08010019 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried cruller data as secondary reference. Trans fat estimated from deep-frying process literature. GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database for fried refined wheat dough products. Overall confidence is high for the HPB-sourced macros; secondary estimates are clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried cruller/you tiao (NDB 18249) applied proportionally to the fat fraction reported by HPB; not directly measured for this composite dish. 2. Trans fat (0.5g/100g) is an estimate based on deep-frying process for you tiao using partially hydrogenated or recycled frying oil, consistent with published literature on deep-fried dough products in Southeast Asia; not directly measured. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite Chinese rojak dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Chinese rojak with you tiao exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. The input record incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'western' and ethnic_tags as ['western']; corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese' based on culinary origin. 6. The input record listed 'plant_based' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as the dish contains prawn paste (crustacean-derived). 7. Halal status is false due to prawn paste (hae ko) which is a fermented crustacean product; this dish is not certified halal.
